title: Microservices

sections.markdown: Microservices split a system into small, independently deployable services owning their own data. You trade in-process complexity for distributed-system complexity — teams can ship independently, but every call is a network call.

sections.code:
sync:   REST / gRPC       (request/response)
async:  Kafka / RabbitMQ  (events, decoupled)
edge:   API Gateway       (auth, routing, rate limits)
resilience: timeout · retry · circuit breaker · bulkhead
observability: logs · metrics · distributed traces (traceId propagated)

sections.markdown: The eternal question: 'How do you handle transactions across services?' You don't — you use the **Saga pattern**: a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions on failure. Choreography (event-driven) or orchestration (a coordinator) — both valid; pick by team preference and observability needs.
Splitting too early ('microservices for a 5-person team'). You'll pay the distributed tax with no wins. Start with a well-modularized monolith; split when team/domain boundaries demand it.

One service owns its DB (no cross-service DB access). Publish events for state changes; use the Outbox pattern to make DB writes and event publishing atomic. Add a service mesh (Istio/Linkerd) for mTLS and traffic policy without app changes.

flashcards.back: Independent deploys — each team ships without coordinating the whole platform.

flashcards.front: How do you handle cross-service transactions?
flashcards.back: The Saga pattern: local transactions + compensating actions (choreography or orchestration).
Write your domain change AND an outbox event in the same DB transaction; a relay publishes events reliably.

quiz.q: The biggest cost of microservices is…
quiz.explain: Every call is a network call; consistency and debugging get harder.

quiz.q: Cross-service transactions are typically handled by…
quiz.explain: Sagas keep locality and avoid distributed 2PC.

challenges.text: Split a monolith into two services (users + orders) with REST between them.
Add timeouts + retries + circuit breaker with Resilience4j.
Implement a Saga for order → payment → shipping with compensations.
Add the Outbox pattern with a Kafka relay so events are never lost.

interviewQuestions: When would you NOT choose microservices?
How do you handle cross-service transactions?
What is a circuit breaker and when to use it?
